coredata.dc:title: The size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in sonochemical reaction field
coredata.dc:creator: Merouani, Slimane
Hamdaoui, Oualid
coredata.dc:description:
The sonication of aqueous solution generates microscopic cavitation bubbles that may growth and violently collapse to produce highly reactive species (i.e.
OH, HO2
and H2O2), hydrogen and emit light, sonoluminescence. The bubble size is a key parameter that influences the chemical activity of the system. This wok aims to study theoretically the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in ultrasonic cavitation field in water using a single bubble sonochemistry model. The effect of several parameters such as frequency of ultrasound, acoustic intensity and liquid temperature on the range of sonochemically active bubbles for the production of hydrogen was clarified. The numerical simulation results showed that the size of active bubbles is an interval which includes an optimum value at which the production rate of H2 is maximal. It was shown that the range of ambient radius for an active bubble as well as the optimum bubble radius for the production of hydrogen increased with increasing acoustic intensity and decreased with increasing ultrasound frequency and bulk liquid temperature. It was found that the range of ambient bubble radius dependence of the operational conditions followed the same trend as those reported experimentally for sonoluminescing bubbles. Comparison with literature data showed a good agreement between the theoretical determined optimum bubble sizes for the production of hydrogen and the experimental reported sizes for sonoluminescing bubbles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water sonolysis
Cavitation bubbles
Hydrogen production
Size of active bubbles
Computer simulation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid nomenclature primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bubble dynamics and chemical bubble yield
Effect of ambient bubble radius on the production rate of hydrogen
Effect of controllable parameters on the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The calculated results of the liquid pressure (a), the bubble radius (b), the bubble wall velocity (c), the pressure inside a bubble (d) and the temperature inside a bubble (e) as function of time for 4 acoustic periods at 355kHz (conditions − ambient bubble radius: 4.8μm, acoustic intensity: 1Wcm−2 (1.727atm of pressure amplitude), bulk liquid temperature: 20°C). The liquid pressure in (a) is the sum of the acoustic pressure and the static pressure (1atm). The vertical axis in (d) is in logarithmic scale.

Evolution of the reaction system inside a bubble as function of time at around the end of the first bubble collapse (∼2.395μs) at 355kHz for the same conditions as in Fig. 1. The horizontal axis is presented for only ∼0.08μs. The principal vertical axis (number of moles×1015) is in logarithmic scale.

Normalized production rate of H2 for various ambient bubble radius at 355kHz (conditions − acoustic intensity: 1Wcm−2 (1.727atm of pressure amplitude), liquid temperature: 20°C). The horizontal and vertical axes are in logarithmic scale. The insert is the same figure in normal axes.

Normalized amount of vapor trapped at the collapse and the maximum bubble temperature for various ambient bubble radii at 355kHz (conditions − acoustic intensity: 1Wcm−2 (1.727atm of pressure amplitude), liquid temperature: 20°C).

Effect of ultrasonic frequency on the size of sonochemically active bubbles for the production of H2 (conditions − acoustic intensity: 1Wcm−2 (1.727atm of pressure amplitude), liquid temperature: 20°C). The horizontal and vertical axes are in logarithmic scale. The production rate values are normalized with respect to the maximum value obtained at each frequency.
Comparison between the mean experimental bubble radii and the optimum bubble radii obtained in this study for various ultrasound frequencies.
Effect of acoustic intensity on the size (the range and the optimum ambient radius) of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen (conditions− frequency: 140–1100kHz, liquid temperature: 20°C).

row.entry: Range of active ambient bubble radius (μm)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of bulk liquid temperature on the size (the range and the optimum ambient radius) of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen (conditions − frequency: 140–1100kHz, acoustic intensity: 1Wcm−2).

row.entry: Range of active ambient bubble radius (μm)

head.ce:title: The size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in sonochemical reaction field

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Engineering, Department of Process Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Badji Mokhtar – Annaba University, P.O. Box 12, 23000 Annaba, Algeria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Process Engineering, University of Constantine 3, 25000 Constantine, Algeria

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Process Engineering, University of Constantine 3, 25000 Constantine, Algeria.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The sonication of aqueous solution generates microscopic cavitation bubbles that may growth and violently collapse to produce highly reactive species (i.e.
OH, HO2
and H2O2), hydrogen and emit light, sonoluminescence. The bubble size is a key parameter that influences the chemical activity of the system. This wok aims to study theoretically the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in ultrasonic cavitation field in water using a single bubble sonochemistry model. The effect of several parameters such as frequency of ultrasound, acoustic intensity and liquid temperature on the range of sonochemically active bubbles for the production of hydrogen was clarified. The numerical simulation results showed that the size of active bubbles is an interval which includes an optimum value at which the production rate of H2 is maximal. It was shown that the range of ambient radius for an active bubble as well as the optimum bubble radius for the production of hydrogen increased with increasing acoustic intensity and decreased with increasing ultrasound frequency and bulk liquid temperature. It was found that the range of ambient bubble radius dependence of the operational conditions followed the same trend as those reported experimentally for sonoluminescing bubbles. Comparison with literature data showed a good agreement between the theoretical determined optimum bubble sizes for the production of hydrogen and the experimental reported sizes for sonoluminescing bubbles.

ce:def-description.ce:para: speed of sound in the liquid medium, (ms−1)

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen has attracted increased attention as one of the most promising energy source for the future, due to the limited storage of fossil fuels and serious environmental problems [1]. Hydrogen can be produced in many ways from a broad spectrum of initial raw materials. Nowadays, hydrogen is produced predominantly from fossil fuels; roughly 96% of hydrogen is produced by steam reforming of natural gas [2]. Alternative ways could be used to generate H2 such as ethanol gasification [3], water electrolysis [4], biological photosynthesis [5,6], photocatalysis [7–9] and sonolysis. This later could be a promising way to produce clean hydrogen, particularly if water is used as the hydrogen source.

The origin of sonochemistry is acoustic cavitation phenomenon, which is the nucleation, growth and violent collapse of microbubbles (filled with water vapor and dissolved gases) in a fluid subjected to ultrasound [10]. The quick collapse of these microbubbles is nearly adiabatic, yielding temperature of several thousands of Kelvin and pressure of several hundreds of atmospheres inside the bubble [11,12]. Under this dramatic situation, the trapped molecules in the bubble can be brought to an excited-state and dissociate. In the case of aqueous solution, various species such as
OH, HO2
, H
, O, H2O2 and H2 are created from H2O and O2 dissociation and their associate reactions inside the bubble [13,14]. In the case argon-saturated water, the main products of sonolysis were hydrogen peroxide and hydrogen. This later (H2) may be formed at rate of 10–15μMmin−1 [15–17]. The sonolysis of acetylene in aqueous phase yielded a much higher amount of H2, >100μMmin−1 [18]. Acoustic cavitation can generate simultaneously strong shear forces, microjets (near solid surfaces), microstreaming and shockwaves [19]. The extreme conditions in the interior of bubble also lead to the emission of light, called sonoluminescence (SL), which is manifested in two forms: multibubble sonoluminescence (MBSL) and single-bubble sonoluminescence (SBSL) [20,21]. The mechanism of the light emission is still under debate. Many researchers [22–24] reported that the SBSL originates from plasma emissions inside a bubble; thermal bremsstrahlung of free electrons and radiative recombination of electrons and ions. For MBSL, however, some researchers [25,26] suggested the same mechanism as SBSL and others have suggested other mechanisms such as molecular emissions from gases [27] and chemiluminescence of
OH radical inside bubbles [28].

One of the most fundamental parameters which characterize the sonochemical reaction field is the size of bubbles that can undergo inertial collapse to produce reactive species, sonoluminescence and hydrogen. The measurement of this parameter is difficult due to the chaotic nature of acoustic cavitation. There have been several studies [29–34] investigating the cavitation bubble size using different techniques, with the majority conducted at 20kHz. Recently, Brotchie et al. [35] investigated the effects of ultrasound frequency and applied acoustic power on the size distribution of sonoluminescing bubbles in water using a pulsed ultrasound method. They found that the mean bubble size increased with increasing acoustic power and decreased with increasing ultrasound frequency. However, to the best of our knowledge, the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in ultrasonic cavitating field was never addressed neither experimentally nor theoretically. In our recent paper [36], a model for single bubble sonochemistry has been combined with material balances in the aqueous phase for clarifying the mechanism of sonolysis induced-hydrogen production. The results from this study showed that H2 is produced mainly in the gas phase of the bubbles via the recombination of the primarily radicals (H
and
OH) formed by splitting of water vapor molecules under the high temperature developed at the collapse phase of the bubble. In the present work, the early developed model for single bubble sonochemistry have been used for predicting the size (in term of ambient bubble radius) of sonochemically active bubbles for the production of hydrogen. Computer simulations were conducted for bubbles oscillating in water saturated with argon. The effect of various experimental parameters including ultrasonic frequency (140–1100kHz), acoustic intensity (0.75 and 1Wcm−2) and liquid temperature (20–50°C) on the size of actives bubbles for the production of H2 were clarified.

The model adopted in the present numerical simulations have been fully described in Refs. [36–39]. It combines the dynamic of single bubble in acoustic field with chemical kinetics consisting of series of chemical reactions occurring in the bubble at the collapse phase. The following is a brief description of the model.

A gas and vapor filled spherical bubble isolated in water oscillates under the action of a sinusoidal sound wave. The temperature and pressure in the bubble are assumed to be spatially uniform and the gas content of the bubble behaves as an ideal gas [40]. The radial dynamics of the bubble is described by the Keller–Miksis equation that includes first order terms in the Mach number
M =
R
̇
/ c
[41]:
(1)
1 -
R
̇
c
R
R
¨
+
3
2
1 -
R
̇
3 c
R
̇
2
=
1
ρ
L
1 +
R
̇
c
+
R
c
d
dt
p -
p
∞
-
2 σ
R
- 4 μ
R
̇
R
-
p
s
( t )
in this equation dots denote time derivatives (d/dt), R is the radius of the bubble, c is the speed of sound in the liquid, ρL is the density of the liquid, σ is the surface tension, μ is the liquid viscosity, p is the pressure inside the bubble, p ∞ is the ambient static pressure and ps (t) is the time-dependent pressure field driving the bubble into oscillation. In the case of bath-type sonochemical reactor, actual acoustic field in the reactor is a damped standing-wave field [42]. However, when a bubble is irradiated by an acoustic wave whose wavelength is much longer than the bubble radius, the acoustic pressure can be described assuming a traveling-wave field as follows [38]: ps (t)=−PA sin(2πft), where PA is the acoustic amplitude of the acoustic wave and f is its frequency. The acoustic amplitude PA is correlated with the acoustic intensity Ia , or power per unit area, as PA
=(2IaρLc)1/2 [43].

The expansion of the bubble is assumed as isothermal and its total compression is considered as adiabatic [44]. These assumptions, which are widely accepted since the lifetime of an oscillation at high frequency is relatively short with a very rapidly occurring collapse event, were pointed out by Yasui et al. [45] using a more detailed model. We also assume that the vapor pressure in the bubble remains constant during the bubble expansion phase and there is no gas diffusion during expansion and no mass and heat transfer of any kind during collapse. Storey and Szeri [46] demonstrated that the inclusion of mass transfer on the bubble dynamics has practically no effect on the maximum bubble temperature attained in the bubble at the collapse when the compression ratio of the bubble (R max/R min) is less than 20 (R max is the maximum radius of the bubble and R min is the minimum bubble radius at the collapse). This level of R max/R min was never attained in the present numerical study. Therefore, in order to reduce computational parameters, the current model takes, as input, initial bubble vapor content and neglects mass and heat transfer during bubble expansion and collapse.

Based on the above assumptions, the pressure and temperature inside the bubble at any instant during the collapse phase can be calculated from the bubble size as
(2)

where Pv is the vapor pressure, Pg
0 = p ∞ +(2σ/R 0)− Pv is the gas pressure in the bubble at its ambient state (R = R 0), R 0 is the ambient bubble radius, T ∞ is the bulk liquid temperature and γ is the ratio of specific heats capacities (cp /cv ) of the gas/vapor mixture.

A kinetic mechanism consisting in 25 chemical reactions and their backwards reactions (first 25 reactions of Table 2 in Ref. [47]) is taken into account involving Ar, O2, H2O,
OH, H
, O, HO2
, H2 and H2O2 species. Rate expressions for the chemical reactions consider elementary reversible reactions involving k chemical species, which can be represented in the general form as
(4)

in which υki in the stoichiometric coefficients of the ith reaction and Xk is the chemical symbol for the kth species. The superscript ′ indicates forward stoichiometric coefficients, while ″ indicates reverse stoichiometric coefficients. The production rate
w

where Rg is the universal gas constant, A fi (Ari ) is the pre-exponential factor, bfi (bri ) is the temperature exponent and Efi (Eri ) is the activation energy. Arrhenius parameters of each reaction of the adopted scheme are available [47].

The Keller–Miksis equation (Eq. (1)), describing the dynamic of the bubble, is a non-linear second-order differential equation which requires an approximate numerical method for solution. Eq. (1) can be reduced to a system of two differential first-order equations
(9)

The system of Eqs. (9) and (10) was solved by the fourth-order Runge–Kutta method using the following initial conditions:

t =0, R = R 0 and
R
̇
= 0

The simulation of the chemical reactions in the bubble starts at the beginning of the adiabatic phase (at time corresponding to R = R max). The application of Eq. (5) for all species involved in the reaction scheme gives a system of ten ordinary differential equations. For example, the application of Eq. (5) to the H2O species gives:
(11)

when V is the volume of the bubble and n H2O is the number of moles of H2O.

Using the ideal-gas law PV = ntRgT, Eq. (11) can be rewritten as

OH ] }
where nt is number of mole of all species present in the bubble.

The input parameters for solving the system of the ordinary differential equations obtained by Eq. (5) are the composition of the bubble on water vapor and argon at time corresponding to R = R max, the temperature and pressure profiles in the bubble during adiabatic phase and the collapse time. These parameters are obtained by solving the dynamic equation (Eq. (1)). As the bubble temperature increases during the adiabatic phase, the reaction system evolves and radicals start to form by thermal dissociation of H2O in the bubble. Thus, the composition of the bubble on all species expected to be present was determined at any temperature during the collapse period by solving the system of the ordinary differential equations obtained by Eq. (5). The duration of the reaction simulation is around the first collapse time of the bubble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bubble dynamics and chemical bubble yield
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 1 (a)−(e), the calculated results of the bubble oscillation at 355kHz of frequency, 1Wcm−1 of acoustic intensity (1.727atm of pressure amplitude) and 20°C of liquid temperature have been shown for 4 acoustic periods (12μs). The liquid pressure applied on the bubble (Fig. 1(a)) is the sum of the acoustic pressure and the static pressure (1atm) [48]. In Fig. 1(b), the bubble radius is shown. The ambient bubble radius (R 0) is chosen as 4.8μm because it is the typical value for active bubbles at 355kHz [49]. Starting from R 0, as the pressure amplitude (1.727atm) is larger than the ambient pressure (1atm), the liquid pressure becomes negative at the rarefaction phase and the bubble expands very much up to a maximum radius of ∼12μm. At the compression phase, the liquid pressure becomes greater than 1atm, the bubble expansion slows down and the bubble collapses very violently. The gas density in the bubble increased suddenly up to ∼17.4kgm−3 at the end of the implosion. At this point, the repulsive forces between molecules and atoms (Van der Waals repulsion forces) prevent the bubble to be shrunk more [50]. As a result, the implosion stops immediately and the bubble expands again during the following rarefaction cycle. As mentioned in the introduction, many physical events, i.e. microstreaming and shockwaves, may accompany the bubble collapse. These phenomenons may affect the bubble dynamics, and thus the bubble size. In this study, due to the complex nature of these phenomena, their effect on the bubble dynamics has been ignored.

In Fig. 1(c), the bubble wall velocity during the bubble oscillation has been shown. The speed of the bubble wall increased drastically during the final stage of the bubble collapse and high values, ∼55ms−1, have been predicted. These high speeds of implosion generate stronger collapses, which yielded enormous conditions inside the bubbles. Indeed, the bubble internal pressure and temperature may increase up to 150atm and 4200K at the end of the bubble collapse, as can be seen in Fig. 1(d) and (e), respectively. Such extreme conditions produced inside the bubble providing a unique environment where high-energy chemical reactions occur as can be seen in Fig. 2 , which is an enlargement view of the chemical reactions occurring in the bubble at the final stage of the first bubble collapse. When the temperature inside the bubble increased during the final stage of the bubble collapse, chemical reaction occurred in time scale of ∼0.05μs and many chemical products such as H2, O2, H2O2,
OH, O, H
and HO2
are created from the thermal dissociation of water vapor and gases molecules and their associate reactions (Fig. 2). From this figure, it is clearly observed that H2 is the first and the dominant molecular product of the pyrolysis chemistry inside the bubble. The amount of hydrogen attained its upper limit at the end of the bubble collapse, followed almost by constant production as the bubble temperature and pressure decreased very soon after this point.

By analyzing the chemical kinetic results, it was found that, for all simulation conditions, more than 99.9% of H2 were produced by the reaction H
+
OH↔H2 +O (see also Refs. [36,39]). Therefore, H2 was formed in the bubble mainly from the recombination of the primary radicals created in the bubble (H
,
OH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ambient bubble radius on the production rate of hydrogen
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to studied the effect of ambient bubble radius on the production rate of hydrogen, computer simulation of bubble oscillation and chemical reaction occurring therein at 355kHz of ultrasonic frequency, 1Wcm−2 of acoustic intensity (1.727atm of pressure amplitude) and 20°C of liquid temperature have been performed for various ambient bubble radii. The obtained results are depicted in Fig. 3 in term of normalized production rate of H2 versus ambient bubble radius (R 0). The notion of production rate (in mol/s), which is defined as the amount of hydrogen formed inside a bubble at the end of the first bubble collapse multiplied by ultrasonic frequency [51], have been used for comparing the yield obtained with different bubble radii per second because of the difficulty in determining the number of oscillation occurs by each bubble. The vertical axes in these figures are in logarithmic scale. It is seen from Fig. 3 that the production of H2 occurs at a maximum rate in bubbles of ambient radius of about 4.8μm; at lower and higher ambient radii, the production rate decreases and finally reaches the threshold limit of the production (10−22 mol [51,52]), at 0.8μm (the lower bound) and 8.58μm (the upper bound). This behavior, which was observed for all other conditions of frequency, intensity and liquid temperature, is in agreement with that reported for the production of the oxidants (
OH, H2O2 …) [51,52]. Experimentally, Brotchie et al. [35] and Pflieger et al. [49] showed that the bubble size distribution of sonoluminescing bubbles in acoustic cavitation field induced by high frequency ultrasound have a Gaussian form with the existence of an optimum ambient bubble radius, which is known as the mean ambient bubble radius. The same behavior was observed at low frequency (20kHz) by Burdin et al. [29] and Tsochatzidis et al. [31].

The interpretation of the existence of an optimal radius for the production of hydrogen is the same as that generated in our previous paper [52] for the production of radicals as H2 is a product of the radicals’ recombination reaction (H
+
OH↔H2 +O [36,39]). Briefly, the optimum radius results mainly from the competition between two factors: the amount of water vapor trapped in the bubble and the maximum temperature reached in the bubble at the collapse. These two parameters are shown versus the ambient bubble radius in Fig. 4 for the same conditions as in Fig. 3. From Fig. 4, it can be observed that the bubble temperature as well as the amount of water vapor proportionally increased with the ambient bubble radius from 0.9 to 2μm. The result is an enhancement in the production rate of radicals (produced from the dissociation of water vapor molecules) and this enhanced the production rate of hydrogen, as observed in Fig. 3. Above 2μm, the higher amount of water vapor starts to reduce the bubble temperature through the decrease of the heat capacity of the gas/vapor mixture inside the bubble. However, the production rate of H2 continuous to increase up to 4.8μm, despite the temperature decrease in this region. This is due to the fact that higher bubble temperatures are generated in this region (2–4.8μm) and with the excess of water vapor more radicals will be formed yielding, as consequence, higher amount of hydrogen. Above 4.8μm, the production rate of hydrogen starts to decrease due to the sharp decrease in the maximum bubble temperature due to the endothermic effect of water vapor, as shown in Fig. 4.

Consequently, there exists an optimum balance between the higher temperatures of the collapse and the amount of water vapor trapped in the bubble at which the production of radicals, and thus hydrogen, attained their upper limit. According to our numerical simulations, this optimum balance occurs for bubbles of 4.8μm in ambient radius at 355kHz and 1Wcm−2. Surprisingly, this value, 4.8μm, is exactly the same experimental size estimated for sonoluminescing bubbles [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of controllable parameters on the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the controllable parameters dependence of the size of active bubble for the production of hydrogen, numerical simulations of bubble oscillation and chemical reactions therein have been performed at various ambient bubble radii for various ultrasonic frequencies, different acoustic intensities and diverse liquid temperatures.

The effect of ultrasonic frequency in the range of 140–1140kHz on the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen is depicted in Fig. 5 in term of normalized production rate of H2 versus ambient bubble radius. The selected frequencies were chosen because they are usually used for sonochemistry and are those where some cavitation size for sonoluminescing bubbles were experimentally determined. The values are normalized with respect to the maximum value obtained at each frequency. From this figure, it is quite clear that as the ultrasound frequency increases, the range of ambient radius for an active bubble for the production of H2 becomes less wideness and the optimal ambient radius becomes smallest. Experimentally, in addition to the size distribution decreasing with increasing acoustic frequency for sonoluminescing bubbles, Brotchie et al. [35] also observed that the main bubble size decreases as the frequency of ultrasound increases. However, the experimental results showed narrowness in ranges of ambient bubble radius for sonoluminescing bubbles (i.e. 3–5.75μm at 355kHz [35]) compared to that obtained in our study for the production of hydrogen. Yasui et al. [51] reported that the range of ambient bubble radius for the production of the oxidants (sonochemically active bubbles) is wider than that predicted for sonoluminescing bubbles. This is in fact because temperature requirements for sonochemical reactions and sonoluminescence are quite different. Sonochemistry (bond dissociation) requires only modest bubble temperatures whereas sonoluminescence (ionization) will only occur under much hotter core conditions [35]. Sonochemistry will therefore occur well below the threshold temperature for sonoluminescence emission, which can be attained by smaller cavitation bubbles [35]. It should be noted that the acoustic intensity used in our system is much higher than that employed in the experimental reported data.

Table 1
regroups the optimal bubble radii, determined in this study, and some reported experimental main ambient bubble radii for sonoluminescing bubbles as function of ultrasonic frequency. As can be seen, an excellent concordance between the experimental main ambient radii for sonoluminescing bubbles and the theoretical optimum radii for the production of H2 was observed. This is the most important statement that can be made from this study on the effect of frequency on the size of active bubble for the production of hydrogen.

The effect of acoustic intensity (0.75 and 1Wcm−2) on the size (the range and the optimum radius) of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen in sonochemical reaction field is shown in Table 2 at various frequencies (140–1140kHz). As can be observed, the rise in acoustic intensity from 0.75 to 1Wcm−2 resulted in increasing the size of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen. Other theoretical works predicted the same behavior for sonoluminescing [51] and sonochemically [37] active bubbles. Labouret and Frohly [54] showed experimentally at 350kHz and 1.1MHz that the bubble size distribution in acoustic cavitation field becomes more wideness and the mean bubble size becomes greater at higher acoustic intensities. Similarly, Brotchie et al. [35] and Barber et al. [56] reported that the size of sonoluminescing bubbles increases with increasing acoustic power.

The largest interval of ambient bubble radius for the production of hydrogen at higher acoustic intensity is generally expected as higher bubble core temperatures, higher trapped amount of water vapor and larger collapse time have been predicted at higher acoustic intensity [39]. The increase of all these parameters with increasing acoustic intensity promotes the formation of free radicals since they result essentially from the dissociation of the water vapor molecules inside the bubble. This implies that the implosion of the largest bubbles, which were not active at low acoustic intensity, will be stronger at high acoustic intensity and, so, these bubbles become active and generate H2 as a result of radicals’ recombination.

Table 3 regroups the results of the liquid temperature (20 and 50°C) effect on the size (the range and the optimum radius) of active bubbles for the production of hydrogen. These results were given at 1Wcm−2 of acoustic intensity for various frequencies (140–1140kHz). It was seen that the rise in liquid temperature from 20 to 50°C resulted in significant reduction in the size of active bubble for the production of hydrogen. These results may be interpreted on the basis of our previous paper [39]: the rise of liquid temperature resulted in more water vapor being trapped in the bubbles at the collapse as a consequence of the rise of liquid vapor pressure with heating. This involves less violent collapses (decreasing γ of the gas mixture) leading to lower internal temperature at the collapse, which reduces the decomposition of molecules into free radicals conducting to lower production of hydrogen (the quantitative detail is available in Ref. [39]). This implies that active bubbles of lower internal temperatures (near the higher and lower bounds) will be inactive when the liquid temperature was increased to 50°C. Experimental studies concerning the effect of liquid temperature on the size of active bubble is scare and thus the comparison cannot be made to support the obtained results.

In this work, we theoretically studied, for the first time, the size of active bubbles (in term of ambient bubble radius) for the production of hydrogen in sonochemical reaction field induced by high frequency ultrasound. The computer simulations of chemical reactions inside the bubbles during their strong collapses revealed that the size of active bubble for the production of H2 is a range inside it there exists an optimum size at which the production rate of hydrogen is maximal. It was found that the range of active bubbles size as well as the optimum size decreased significantly with increasing the frequency of ultrasound and liquid temperature and increased with increasing the acoustic intensity. Comparison with literature data showed a good agreement between the theoretical determined optimum bubble sizes for the production of hydrogen and the experimental reported sizes for sonoluminescing bubbles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research of Algeria (project No. A16N01UN230120130010) is greatly acknowledged.
